title: How to Use Intuition When Evaluating Advice
meta_description: Stop outsourcing your decisions. Learn to trust your gut when evaluating advice. This article shows you how to separate intuition from fear.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Evaluating Advice
og_description: Stop outsourcing your decisions. Learn to trust your gut when evaluating advice. This article shows you how to separate intuition from fear.
body:
You're Drowning in Advice. Here's How to Actually Use Your Gut.

You know that feeling. Someone gives you advice - maybe it's a friend, a mentor, a parent, or a complete stranger on the internet - and something in your chest tightens. Or maybe it's a quiet "no" that settles in your stomach like a stone. Or maybe it's a sudden lightness, a sense of "yes" that feels like relief.

That's your intuition. And if you're reading this, you probably already know it's there. The problem isn't that you can't feel it. The problem is that you've been trained to ignore it.

You've been told to be "reasonable." To make lists of pros and cons. To ask five people for their opinion and then average them out like you're calculating a grade. You've been told that your feelings are unreliable, that you're "too sensitive," that you need to think more and feel less.

Bullshit.

Your intuition isn't some vague, mystical woo-woo thing. It's your nervous system processing information faster than your conscious mind can keep up. It's the sum total of every experience you've ever had, every boundary you've ever crossed, every time you ignored a red flag and regretted it. It's a survival mechanism. And when it comes to evaluating advice - especially advice about your life, your work, your relationships - it's the most reliable tool you've got.

But here's the thing: you can't just "trust your gut" blindly. You need to learn how to use it. You need to separate the signal from the noise. You need to know when your intuition is speaking and when it's just fear wearing a cheap costume. Right?!

So let's talk about how to actually do that. Let's talk about how to take all that advice coming at you from every direction and filter it through something that actually knows you - your own body, your own knowing, your own deep sense of what's true.

"Your intuition isn't a magic eight ball. It's a conversation with the part of you that already knows the answer but has been told to keep quiet."

Why You Can't Just Trust Your Gut (Yet)

I know, I know. I just told you your intuition is reliable. And it is. But here's the catch: your intuition is also messy. It's tangled up with your trauma, your conditioning, your fear of disappointing people, your desire to be seen as smart or successful. It's not a pure, clear channel. It's a river that's been dammed up in a hundred places by a hundred different things you've been told about who you're supposed to be.

So when someone gives you advice and you feel that immediate "no" in your chest, you need to ask yourself: is that a true "no," or is it fear? Is it your intuition protecting you, or is it your ego protecting its story about who you are?

Here's a quick way to tell the difference. A true intuitive "no" feels grounded. It feels like a door closing, but not a slammed door - more like a door that was never meant to open. It's quiet. It's certain. It doesn't need to argue or justify itself. It just is.

Fear, on the other hand, is loud. It's frantic. It comes with a story: "But what if I fail? What if I look stupid? What if they're right and I'm wrong?" Fear is the voice that keeps you small. Intuition is the voice that keeps you you.

Does that land? Because this distinction is everything. If you can't tell the difference between fear and intuition, you'll spend your life either ignoring good advice or following bad advice out of obligation. Neither one is freedom.

The Body Test: Your First Filter for Any Advice

Here's the simplest, most practical tool I know for evaluating advice. I call it the Body Test. You can do it in thirty seconds, anywhere, with anyone.

When someone gives you advice, don't think about it. Don't analyze it. Don't start making a list of pros and cons. Instead, take a breath and drop your attention into your body. Notice what happens.

  • Does your chest feel tight or open?
  • Does your stomach clench or relax?
  • Do your shoulders rise toward your ears, or do they drop?
  • Do you feel a sense of expansion, like you're being given permission to grow?
  • Or do you feel a sense of contraction, like you're being boxed in?

This isn't complicated. Your body is honest. It doesn't lie. It doesn't care about being polite. It doesn't care about what you "should" do. It only knows what's safe and what's not safe, what's aligned and what's not aligned, what's true for you and what's someone else's story.

Now, here's the tricky part. Sometimes your body will contract because the advice is true and you don't want to hear it. That's different. That contraction feels sharp, like a pinch. It's the feeling of being called out, of being seen, of being asked to grow. That's not a "no" - that's a "yes" that hurts. And it's worth paying attention to.

But if the contraction feels dull, heavy, or suffocating - like a weight settling on your chest - that's your intuition telling you this advice is not for you. It might be great advice for someone else. It might be objectively correct. But it's not your truth.

The Three Questions: A Deeper Filter

Okay, so you've done the Body Test. You've felt into the advice. Now what?

Now you ask three questions. And you ask them with your body, not your head. You don't answer them with logic. You answer them with sensation.

Question One: Does this advice honor my sovereignty?

Does it treat you like the authority of your own life? Or does it assume that someone else knows better than you? Real advice empowers you to make your own decision. Bad advice tells you what to do and expects you to comply. You can feel the difference. Advice that honors your sovereignty leaves you feeling more capable, not less. It opens a door and invites you to walk through. It doesn't push you.

Question Two: Does this advice come from someone who has walked the path I'm walking?

This one is huge. I can't tell you how many times I've taken advice from people who had no business giving it. People who had never been through what I was going through. People who were speaking from theory, not experience. People who were projecting their own fears onto me.

You can feel the difference. Advice from someone who has actually lived it has a certain weight. It's grounded. It's specific. It doesn't use generic platitudes. It says things like, "Here's what I tried, here's what worked, here's what didn't." Advice from someone who hasn't lived it is vague. It sounds like a fortune cookie. It might even be correct in the abstract, but it doesn't land in your body as true.

Question Three: What would it cost me to follow this advice?

Every choice has a cost. Even good choices. Even the "right" choices. Following advice means saying no to something else - maybe your own instinct, maybe a different path, maybe the version of yourself that would have figured it out on your own. What's the cost of following this advice? And are you willing to pay it?

Your intuition already knows the answer to this question. It's been doing the math in the background, weighing the trade-offs, sensing the future. All you have to do is stop and listen.

When to Ignore Advice Entirely

Here's something nobody tells you about advice: you're allowed to ignore all of it. You're allowed to hear someone out, thank them for their input, and then do exactly what you were going to do anyway. You don't owe anyone compliance just because they offered their opinion.

And here's something else: some advice is actually harmful. Not just unhelpful - actively harmful. Advice that tells you to suppress your feelings. Advice that tells you to "just get over it." Advice that tells you to be smaller, quieter, more agreeable. Advice that comes from someone who is threatened by your growth.

You can feel this too. It's that sinking sensation in your gut. That feeling of being diminished. That quiet voice that says, "Something about this isn't right."

Trust that voice. It's not being dramatic. It's being protective.

I've written a whole book about this process - about learning to trust your own knowing even when the whole world is telling you otherwise. It's called The Gift of Being Sensitive, and it's for people who have been told their whole lives that they feel too much. If that sounds like you, you might want to check it out. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Difference Between Intuition and Projection

This is the thing that trips most people up. You think you're listening to your intuition, but you're actually listening to someone else's voice that you've internalized. Your mother's voice. Your father's voice. The voice of the culture that told you to be a certain way.

That's not intuition. That's conditioning. And conditioning is loud. It's repetitive. It sounds like a broken record. It says the same things over and over: "You're not good enough. You need to try harder. You should be more like so-and-so. Don't rock the boat."

Intuition doesn't sound like that. Intuition is quiet. It's original. It surprises you. It says things like, "Actually, you don't have to take that job." Or, "That relationship is over, and it's okay to let go." Or, "You know more than you think you do."

So how do you tell the difference? You practice. You start noticing which voices in your head are familiar and which ones feel new. You start asking, "Whose voice is this?" and waiting for the answer. And you start giving yourself permission to follow the quiet voice, even when it's scary.

There's a book that changed everything for me on this topic. It's called The Untethered Soul, and it's about learning to separate your awareness from the noise in your head. If you struggle with overthinking and second-guessing, I can't recommend it enough. See on Amazon (paid link)

How to Practice Intuitive Discernment (Without Making Yourself Crazy)

You don't have to be a meditation master to use your intuition. You don't have to sit on a cushion for an hour a day. You just need to practice in small, low-stakes situations.

Start with something simple. The next time someone asks you what you want for lunch, notice your body's response before you answer. Do you actually want the salad, or are you saying "salad" because you think you should? Notice the difference between a "yes" that feels light and a "yes" that feels heavy.

Then move to slightly bigger things. When someone invites you to an event, check in with your body before you respond. Do you feel open or closed? Do you feel excited or obligated? Let your body answer before your mouth does.

Then move to the big stuff. Career decisions. Relationship decisions. Life-changing advice from people you respect. By the time you get to these, you'll have built the muscle. You'll know what your "yes" feels like and what your "no" feels like. You'll be able to trust yourself.

And here's the secret: the more you trust yourself, the less advice you actually need. Because you start to realize that most of the time, you already know the answer. You're just looking for permission. And the only person who can give you that permission is you.

What to Do When You're Genuinely Confused

Sometimes you do all the body checks, ask all the questions, and you still don't know. You're genuinely torn. Two pieces of advice seem equally valid. Your intuition feels silent. Now what?

First, don't panic. Silence isn't a problem. It's information. It might mean that both options are fine. It might mean that neither option is right and you need to wait for a third one. It might mean that you're not ready to decide yet, and that's okay.

Second, take the pressure off. You don't have to decide today. You don't have to decide tomorrow. You can sit with the question and let it marinate. You can say, "I need some time to think about this," and actually take that time.

Third, do something unrelated. Go for a walk. Take a shower. Cook a meal. Your intuition works best when you're not trying to force it. It's like trying to remember a word that's on the tip of your tongue - the harder you try, the more it eludes you. Let it go, and it will come back.

Fourth, try the "flip a coin" trick. Assign one option to heads and one to tails. Flip the coin. Don't look at it. Notice which side you're hoping it landed on. That's your answer.

This works because your intuition doesn't speak in words. It speaks in desire. It speaks in preference. It speaks in the subtle shift of your attention toward one option and away from the other. The coin flip is just a way to surface what you already know.

If you want to go deeper into this process, there's a book called The Surrender Experiment that I've found incredibly useful. It's about learning to trust life instead of trying to control it. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Bottom Line: You Are the Authority

Here's what I want you to take away from this. You are not broken. You are not too sensitive. You are not bad at making decisions. You have been trained to doubt yourself, and that training can be undone.

Your intuition is not a luxury. It's not a nice-to-have. It's the most practical tool you have for navigating a world that is constantly trying to tell you who to be and what to do. When you learn to use it, you stop wasting years of your life following advice that was never meant for you. You stop second-guessing yourself. You stop looking for someone else to give you permission.

You become the authority of your own life.

And that doesn't mean you stop listening to other people. It means you listen differently. You take what's useful and leave the rest. You honor the wisdom of others without giving away your own power. You become a discerning receiver of advice, not a passive consumer of it.

There's one more book I want to mention. It's called The Power of Now, and it's about learning to be present with what is, rather than getting lost in your thoughts about what should be. For anyone who struggles with anxiety and overthinking, it's a lifeline. See on Amazon (paid link)

So here's your invitation. The next time someone gives you advice - even good advice, even well-meaning advice - pause. Take a breath. Drop into your body. Ask yourself: does this feel true? Not "is this correct?" Not "does this make sense?" But: does this feel true in my bones?

And then trust what you find.

You already know the way. You just have to stop pretending you don't.

If you're ready to go deeper into this work, I've created a free assessment that helps you identify where you're giving away your power and how to take it back. It takes about ten minutes and it might change everything. Take the assessment here.
